@@ -1581,6 +1581,17 @@ def test_iterable_dataset_set_epoch(dataset: IterableDataset):
     assert dataset._epoch == 42
 
 
+def test_iterable_dataset_set_epoch_resuming(dataset: IterableDataset):
+    dataset_length = len(list(dataset))
+    assert len(list(dataset)) == dataset_length > 0
+    dataset.load_state_dict(dataset.state_dict())
+    assert len(list(dataset)) == 0
+    dataset.set_epoch(1)
+    assert len(list(dataset)) == dataset_length > 0
+    dataset.load_state_dict(dataset.state_dict())
+    assert len(list(dataset)) == 0
+
+
 @pytest.mark.parametrize("seed", [None, 42, 1337])
 @pytest.mark.parametrize("epoch", [None, 0, 1, 10])
 def test_iterable_dataset_set_epoch_of_shuffled_dataset(dataset: IterableDataset, seed, epoch):

steps.operation: Refactor dict construction to use dict.fromkeys for mapping multiple TFDS imports to the same output directory
steps.reason: Simplify and standardize creation of dicts with shared values, improving readability and avoiding repeated comprehension patterns.
steps.side_effects: Only affects how the temporary imports-to-builder map is created during conversion; downstream conversion behavior should remain identical but could subtly affect ordering/debug output if callers relied on insertion behavior.

steps.operation: Refactor default cache_file_names initialization to use dict.fromkeys(self)
steps.reason: Make default per-split dict initialization more idiomatic/consistent and slightly clearer when values are uniformly None.
steps.side_effects: Affects the default structure passed into per-split Dataset.map calls; behavior should be unchanged unless external code depended on the exact dict-construction style.

steps.operation: Refactor default cache_file_names initialization to use dict.fromkeys(self)
steps.reason: Keep consistent default dict initialization across DatasetDict transformation methods.
steps.side_effects: Only impacts default arguments plumbing into Dataset.filter per split; no expected functional change.

steps.operation: Refactor default cache_file_names initialization to use dict.fromkeys(self)
steps.reason: Improve readability and consistency for default per-split cache file naming.
steps.side_effects: May affect callers that introspect the passed cache_file_names object identity, but functional output remains the same.

steps.operation: Refactor default indices_cache_file_names initialization to use dict.fromkeys(self)
steps.reason: Standardize how per-split defaults are created for index caching across DatasetDict APIs.
steps.side_effects: Only influences how defaults are computed; sorting results and caching semantics should remain unchanged.

steps.operation: Refactor seed/generator/index cache default dict initialization to use dict.fromkeys (including dict.fromkeys(self, seeds) when scalar seed provided)
steps.reason: Make the default dict creation more concise and consistent while preserving behavior for None/scalar/dict seed inputs.
steps.side_effects: Touches argument normalization for shuffle; could affect edge cases where code relied on exact dict literal formatting, but shuffle determinism should remain identical.

steps.operation: Refactor default num_shards initialization to use dict.fromkeys(self)
steps.reason: Consistently initialize per-split num_shards defaults in a concise manner.
steps.side_effects: Only affects default parameter expansion; save-to-disk sharding behavior should not change.

steps.operation: Refactor default num_shards initialization to use dict.fromkeys(self)
steps.reason: Keep parity with save_to_disk and other DatasetDict methods for default dict initialization.
steps.side_effects: Only impacts default shard config expansion before upload; hub push output should remain the same.

steps.operation: Refactor column-name keyed initialization to use dict.fromkeys(pa_table.column_names)
steps.reason: Create a placeholder dict for formatted columns in a more idiomatic/compact way while preserving semantics (values initialized to None).
steps.side_effects: Affects internal LazyBatch.data initialization; formatting behavior should be unchanged unless some code depended on the exact comprehension construction.

steps.operation: Extend base examples-iterable state with a 'type' discriminator field
steps.reason: Embed the iterable class name into the saved state to make serialized states more readable and future-proof for debugging/migrations.
steps.side_effects: State dict schema changes; any external tooling expecting the previous minimal schema may need to ignore/handle the new 'type' field.

steps.operation: Extend generator-based examples-iterable state with a 'type' discriminator field
steps.reason: Keep state dict schema consistent across iterable implementations and improve debuggability.
steps.side_effects: Serialized state size changes slightly; external consumers of the state dict may need to tolerate extra keys.

steps.operation: Extend Arrow-based examples-iterable state with a 'type' discriminator field
steps.reason: Ensure Arrow iteration path produces state dicts compatible with the new top-level schema expectations and debugging patterns.
steps.side_effects: May affect resuming logic if any code asserts exact key sets; otherwise benign.

steps.operation: Extend iterable-with-shards state with a 'type' discriminator field
steps.reason: Make sharded iterable states self-describing (useful when resuming and when nesting multiple iterables).
steps.side_effects: State dict schema changes for sharded iterables; downstream load_state_dict implementations must ignore unknown keys (as expected).

steps.operation: Rename nested state key from 'ex_iterable' to 'examples_iterable' and add 'type' field in state initialization for batched/chunked iterable wrapper
steps.reason: Align naming across the codebase (examples_iterable) and improve readability; prepares for storing wrapper state under a consistent top-level key when resuming across epochs.
steps.side_effects: Breaking change for any previously saved checkpoints that used 'ex_iterable' for this wrapper unless load_state_dict has backward-compat handling elsewhere; nested resume behavior is affected.

steps.operation: Add 'type' field to the interleaving iterable wrapper state initialization
steps.reason: Make complex composite states easier to introspect and debug, especially with multiple child iterables.
steps.side_effects: State dict schema changes for interleave; older states may still load if loader ignores unknown keys.

steps.operation: Add 'type' field to the sequential concatenation/chain iterable wrapper state initialization
steps.reason: Keep all wrappers emitting self-describing state dicts, improving traceability when resuming.
steps.side_effects: Schema change; potential compatibility considerations for any external state consumers.

steps.operation: Add 'type' field to the zip/combine iterable wrapper state initialization
steps.reason: Increase readability of nested states and allow distinguishing wrapper types when debugging resume issues.
steps.side_effects: Schema change only; minimal runtime impact.

steps.operation: Add 'type' field to the round-robin/mux iterable wrapper state initialization
steps.reason: Ensure muxed composite states remain self-describing for resumption and debugging.
steps.side_effects: Schema change; any strict key checking in tests/tools may require updates.

steps.operation: Rename nested state key from 'ex_iterable' to 'examples_iterable' and add 'type' field in skip/take-like wrapper state initialization
steps.reason: Standardize nested iterable state naming and record wrapper type; supports the new top-level state layout used for epoch-aware resuming.
steps.side_effects: Potential incompatibility with previously serialized states that used 'ex_iterable' unless handled elsewhere; resume correctness depends on consistent naming.

steps.operation: Rename nested state key from 'ex_iterable' to 'examples_iterable' and add 'type' field in repeat wrapper state initialization
steps.reason: Ensure repeat wrapper state matches the updated schema and remains self-describing.
steps.side_effects: Older checkpoints for RepeatExamplesIterable may not restore without backward compatibility; impacts resuming behavior.

steps.operation: Update repeat iteration loop to write nested state under 'examples_iterable' instead of 'ex_iterable' when resetting for next repetition
steps.reason: Keep runtime state updates consistent with the renamed key so that state_dict() reflects the actual nested iterable state after changes.
steps.side_effects: Any code expecting the legacy 'ex_iterable' key in mid-iteration state snapshots will break; resuming correctness improves under the new schema.

steps.operation: Rename nested state key from 'ex_iterable' to 'examples_iterable' and add 'type' field in take/limit wrapper state initialization
steps.reason: Unify state naming and make wrapper type explicit; supports correct epoch-aware resuming at higher layers.
steps.side_effects: Potential backward incompatibility for old saved states; affects any consumer that manually edits/reads state dicts.

steps.operation: Refactor IterableDataset initialization to prepare iterables and state dict via _prepare_ex_iterable_for_iteration instead of caching _prepared_ex_iterable and eagerly loading state
steps.reason: Fix resuming behavior when epoch changes: avoid binding a prepared iterable/state too early, and ensure state dict is set in an epoch-aware way during preparation.
steps.side_effects: Lifecycle timing changes: state is now established through preparation calls; any code relying on _prepared_ex_iterable existing immediately after __init__ may break (internal coupling risk).

steps.operation: Change load_state_dict to store the provided state as starting state instead of immediately loading it into a prepared iterable
steps.reason: Enable correct behavior when calling set_epoch(new_epoch): the stored checkpoint should only be applied if the epoch matches, preventing accidental resume into the wrong epoch.
steps.side_effects: State restoration becomes lazy; if callers expected immediate side effects on the active iterator without re-iteration, behavior may differ. Interacts with PyTorch DataLoader resume semantics.

steps.operation: Make PyTorch worker iteration build an epoch-aware top-level state dict and only restore nested iterable state if starting epoch matches current epoch
steps.reason: Fix the bug where resuming after ds.set_epoch(new_epoch) would incorrectly reuse an old state and result in an exhausted dataset; gating restore by epoch ensures a new epoch starts fresh.
steps.side_effects: Checkpoint schema changes (top-level now contains 'examples_iterable' and 'epoch'); existing checkpoints without 'epoch' may fail unless handled elsewhere. Worker sharding/resume behavior changes in PyTorch context.

steps.operation: Update iterable preparation to set epoch-aware state dict and conditionally load starting state only when epochs match
steps.reason: Centralize the corrected resuming logic for both standard and PyTorch iterations: always include epoch in the state and only restore nested state when appropriate.
steps.side_effects: Affects all iteration paths (non-PyTorch and PyTorch if shared); changes the structure returned by state_dict() and how/when starting checkpoints are applied.

steps.operation: Add regression test ensuring resuming works correctly across epoch changes (resume exhausts current epoch, set_epoch resets, resume works again)
steps.reason: Prevent regressions for issue #7447 by asserting that loading the current state exhausts the dataset, and that set_epoch(new_epoch) properly restarts iteration rather than staying exhausted.
steps.side_effects: Test suite now enforces epoch-aware checkpoint semantics; future refactors to state_dict/load_state_dict/set_epoch must preserve these behaviors or update the test accordingly.

step_plan.summary: Fixes IterableDataset resuming semantics so that checkpoints are only applied when the saved epoch matches the current epoch, preventing datasets from staying exhausted after calling set_epoch(new_epoch). Also standardizes state dict readability (adds type fields, renames nested keys) and adds a regression test for the epoch-resume behavior.
